item.title: Interfacial Stability in Single- and Multi-Junction Perovskite Solar Cells
item.description:
Chem. Sci., 2026, Advance Article
DOI: 10.1039/D6SC03324E, Review Article
Open Access
  This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported Licence.
Liangchen Qian, Bohong Chang, Danpeng Gao, Bo Li, Zonglong Zhu
This review summarizes how ion migration, interfacial reactions, and stress/strain govern degradation in single- and multi-junction perovskite solar cells. Chemistry-to-function strategies for stable interfaces are discussed.
To cite this article before page numbers are assigned, use the DOI form of citation above.
The content of this RSS Feed (c) The Royal Society of Chemistry

item.title: Engineering Asymmetric Solvation Structures for Synergistically Boosted Quasi-Solid Thermocells
item.description:
Chem. Sci., 2026, Advance Article
DOI: 10.1039/D6SC03942A, Edge Article
Open Access
  This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported Licence.
Wentao Lin, Shuo Niu, Shukai Wu, Chao Fang
A co-solvent strategy employing trimethyl phosphate and ethylene glycol in hydrogel thermocells overcomes the trade-off between thermopower and ion transport through asymmetric solvation structures for efficient low-grade heat harvesting.
To cite this article before page numbers are assigned, use the DOI form of citation above.

item.description:
CCS Chemistry, Ahead of Print.
DOI:10.31635/ccschem.026.202507170
Membrane curvature orchestrates essential processes such as vesicle fission, fusion, and cell migration, yet its real-time quantification has remained elusive. Here, we introduce the Molecular Tensiometer, a fluorescence-lifetime-based framework for noninvasive, quantitative mapping of membrane curvature using the tension-sensitive probe, Flipper-Tension Reporter (Flipper-TR). A monodisperse liposome library prepared by DNA brick-assisted sorting established a power-law dependence on the probe lifetime of the curvature radius, refined through all-atom molecular dynamics and quantum-chemical analyses. This calibration enabled direct translation of lifetime into curvature with nanoscale precision in model systems, demonstrating that Flipper-TR served as the sensing element of the Molecular Tensiometer. While preserving global morphology, the Molecular Tensiometer resolved nanoscale fluctuations and localized curvature hotspots that are invisible to intensity imaging, providing a high-fidelity platform for probing dynamic membrane mechanics in model lipid membranes.

item.title: Detection of Peroxynitrite with Spatial and Temporal Resolution: Challenges and Opportunities
item.description: The future of precise and site-specific peroxynitrite detection, an important biomarker of oxidative stress, centers on the continued development of (1) selective molecular sensors, (2) their integration into tunable macromolecular architectures, and (3) evaluation across diverse 2D, 3D, and in vivo models.

item.title: Conformationally Gated, Photoswitchable Synthetic Sensors for Visualization of Protein Post-Translational Modification in Vivo
item.description:
CCS Chemistry, Ahead of Print.
DOI:10.31635/ccschem.026.202506798
Biochemical processes in nature feature precision, responsiveness, and controllability, which remain challenging to be mimicked and integrated within a single synthetic sensor. Here, we report a conformation-gated, visible-light-controlled strategy for the design of synthetic peptidyl sensors that couple molecular recognition with optical manipulation and activatable signaling. This platform employed a biomimetic helical peptide as a molecular ruler for spatially defined target engagement and a geometry-sensitive fluorophore that underwent visible-light-driven isomerization to regulate conformation, binding, and fluorescence output. This dual-gated mechanism allowed photoactivation/deactivation of the sensing signal exclusively responding to target molecular events in live systems. As a proof of concept, the sensors achieved selective visualization of linkage-specific protein polyubiquitination in live cells and C. elegans, revealing their role in autophagic cargo processing and its dynamic redistribution within stress granules. Beyond ubiquitination, the modularity of this approach highlights a general chemical strategy for designing light-programmable molecular sensors, expanding the toolkit for probing dynamic and transient biological processes with spatiotemporal precision.

item.title: Manipulating Excited-State Dynamics Through Macrocycle Positioning in a Rotaxane for Sensitive and Discriminative Methanol Sensing
item.description:
Chem. Sci., 2026, 17,12039-12046
DOI: 10.1039/D6SC00753H, Edge Article
Open Access
  This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 3.0 Unported Licence.
Yu Wang, Yalei Ma, Ruijuan Wen, Jing Li, Taihong Liu, Liping Ding, Rong Miao, Yu Fang
A supramolecular strategy for excited-state dynamics manipulation is demonstrated by controlling the distance between the macrocycle and the fluorophore within the rotaxane, which enables sensitive and discriminative methanol vapor sensing.
The content of this RSS Feed (c) The Royal Society of Chemistry

item.title: Array of Bimetallic Metallophthalocyanine-Based Metal-Organic Frameworks for Chemiresistive Detection and Differentiation of Toxic Gases
item.description: This work describes the synthesis, characterization, and fundamental investigation of nine octahydroxyl-linked, two-dimensional conductive metal-organic frameworks (cMOFs) based on metallophthalocyanine (MPc). These cMOFs were fabricated into sensor devices, which demonstrated parts per million (ppm)-level sensitivity toward H2S, NO, and CO and machine-learning-assisted differentiation of gaseous analytes.

item.title: Out-Of-Equilibrium Confinement Catalysis Mediated by Compressive Force
item.description: Mechanical forces play essential roles in living systems, from hearing and touch to cellular mechanosensing. Inspired by this idea, we show that mechanical force can power supramolecular systems. Applying force distorts a molecular cage and lifts it to a higher-energy state, allowing the guest to escape. Coupled with spontaneous reassembly, this process forms a continuous non-equilibrium cycle. This work introduces mechanical force as a programmable energy input for dissipative supramolecular systems.

item.title: Wetting Transparency of Graphene Across Length Scales: Macroscopic Transparency but Nanoscopic Mirror-Like Behavior
item.description: Graphene/water interfaces underpin desalination, sensing, energy, and neuromorphic technologies, yet how substrate-supported graphene modulates interfacial water remains unclear. Using surface-specific vibrational spectroscopy and molecular dynamics, we show that graphene is macroscopically transparent but nanoscopically polarizes to counter substrate electrostatics, reorganizing nearby water. This provides new design rules for controlling interfacial water.

item.title: Out-Of-Equilibrium Sensing with a Chemically Fueled Molecular Machine
item.description: Biological adaptive receptors are driven out of equilibrium to change the detectable range of analyte concentrations. Here, a chemically fueled rotaxane information ratchet couples Zn(II) binding to catalytic carbodiimide hydration through an allosterically shifted macrocycle distribution. Because the catalysis sustains a nonequilibrium steady state, the rate of fuel-to-waste conversion reports Zn(II) concentrations at which the same binding site is saturated at equilibrium, thereby widening the range of analyte detection. This principle should generalize to other adaptive supramolecular sensors and signal transduction systems.

item.title: Multiply Entangled Receptors for High-Affinity Anion Recognition in Water
item.description: Selective sulfate binding in water remains challenging despite its importance in biological and environmental processes. Here, multiply entangled macrocycles are designed to achieve strong sulfate binding in aqueous solution. The performance of these receptors is enhanced by structural entanglement, which enforces three-dimensional preorganization. This study suggests that such receptors could represent a promising platform for anion sensing and capture in water.

item.title: Fully Fused Conjugated Dimeric Heterocycloarenes
item.description:
CCS Chemistry, Ahead of Print.
DOI:10.31635/ccschem.025.202506881
Cycloarenes and heterocycloarenes, characterized by rigid, fully fused π-conjugated structures and shape-persistent cavities, hold significant promise in synthetic chemistry, supramolecular sensors, and semiconductor materials. However, the synthesis of heterocycloarenes with novel structures and specific functions remains a significant challenge in organic and material chemistry. Herein, we report the first case of solution-synthesized dimeric heterocycloarenes (DHC1 and DHC2) with a fully fused, conjugated bismacrocyclic framework via cyclocondensation reactions. The resultant dimeric heterocycloarenes featured two identical heterocycloarene subunits, bridged by a pyrazino[2,3-g]quinoxaline unit. Their bismacrocyclic structures were confirmed in real space at the submolecular level by scanning tunneling microscopy (STM), with the distance between the centers of two macrocycles measured as ∼2.4 nm. Interestingly, due to its particular bismacrocyclic structure and low electron density in the cavities, DHC2 displayed efficient 1:1 binding with iodide ion, tending to form large-sized supramolecular aggregates in solution. Our study opens new avenues for the synthesis of porous carbon nanostructures and lays a foundation for the utilization of heterocycloarene derivatives in supramolecular polymer construction.

item.title: Trace Ionic Liquid-Enhanced Polymer Electrolytes for Low-Voltage Stretchable Field-Effect Transistor Sweat Sensors
item.description:
CCS Chemistry, Ahead of Print.
DOI:10.31635/ccschem.025.202506829
Skin-attachable, stretchable transistors hold significant promise for next-generation wearable biosensors. However, most existing stretchable organic field effect transistors (OFETs) operate at high voltages, raising concerns regarding safety and energy consumption. To address this limitation, we developed a stretchable polymer electrolyte dielectric by blending trace amounts of ionic liquids (ILs) [EMIM]+[TFSI]− into a high-permittivity (κ) polyurethane-urea (PUU) elastomer. This strategy significantly enhances the dielectric capacitance, enabling the stretchable OFETs to operate at ultralow voltages down to −1 V. The resulting ILs-PUU-based stretchable OFETs demonstrate outstanding electrical performance, achieving a high mobility of 25.4 cm2 V−1 s−1, high transconductance of 4.5 × 103 μS mm−1, and robust low-voltage operation. Additionally, we developed extended-gate OFETs capable of detecting specific electrolyte concentrations in human sweat. Our approach paves the way for the development of low-voltage, high-mobility, and stretchable OFET arrays, offering a promising platform for next-generation wearable health monitoring.

item.title: Dearomative Spirocyclized Pillar[5]arenes: Multidimensional Stimuli-Responsive Chiroptical Switches Operable in Both Solution and Solid States
item.description:
CCS Chemistry, Ahead of Print.
DOI:10.31635/ccschem.025.202506868
Controllable conformational switching in the solid state remains challenging due to the inherent rigidity and spatial constraints of the solid environment. Herein, we report a novel class of pillar[5]arene (P[5]) derivatives, a quasi-penguinone-containing P[5] (QPP[5]), featuring a unique spiro[4,5]trienone unit embedded within the P[5] macrocycle. QPP[5] was synthesized via P[5]-facilitated dearomative spirocyclization, whereas the same precursor underwent Glaser coupling without a macrocyclic scaffold, revealing a dramatically switched reaction pathway due to catalyst binding. Enantiopure QPP[5] displayed pronounced exciton-coupled circular dichroism (CD) signals stemming from efficient intramolecular chirality transfer, with g-values exceeding 2% at the charge transfer (CT) band. In solution, QPP[5] underwent reversible chiroptical switching upon guest encapsulation or solvent variation, attributed to interconversion between in/out chiral conformers. Additionally, temperature modulation induced CD signal reversal, reflecting significant entropy changes between these conformers. Notably, in solid state, QPP[5] displayed a large, reversible CD spectral inversion in response to organic vapors or thermal stimuli, an unprecedented single-molecule chiral conformational switch with externally controllable inversion. X-ray analysis revealed a strong correlation between planar chirality of hydroquinone ether units and the penguinone unit, where guest inclusion/release induced complete macrocycle conformational inversion. This arose from small in/out geometric shifts enabled by the inherent flexibility and adaptability of the macrocycle, exhibiting a new molecular platform for solid-state chiroptical materials with potential applications in photonic and sensing devices.

item.title: Enhanced Sensitivity of Near-Infrared Organic Photodetectors via Ternary Compensation for Contact/Contactless Vitality Surveillance
item.description:
CCS Chemistry, Ahead of Print.
DOI:10.31635/ccschem.025.202506094
Near-infrared (NIR) detection is pivotal to the advancement of noninvasive biomedical monitoring. However, simultaneously achieving contact and contactless health detection remains a critical challenge. Although the NIR organic photodetector (OPD) offers intrinsic flexibility for such applications, its low sensitivity, slow response, and poor wavelength selectivity significantly hinders its application in dual-mode detection. Here, we develop a high-performance NIR OPD through a synergistic molecular design and ternary compensation strategy. By introducing a quinoidal π-bridge into a CH-series acceptor, we synthesize a narrow-bandgap acceptor, ZCH-1, realizing a broad spectral absorption ranging from 300 to 1100 nm. Moreover, integrating the donor D18 into the binary OPD effectively passivates trap states and suppresses electron injection, leading to a reduced dark current (8.4 × 10−9 A cm−2), improved responsivity (0.41 A W−1), enhanced specificity detectivity (4.1 × 1012 Jones) at 950 nm, and a rapid response time of 2.4 μs. These metrics represent state-of-the-art balanced NIR OPD for detection beyond 950 nm. Moreover, this device enables real-time physiological measurement in both contact and contactless modes at distances up to 70 mm. Given this performance, coupled with its cost-effective and large-area processing, the high-performance NIR OPD presents a promising platform for next-generation wearable health monitoring and remote healthcare systems.

item.title: Efficient and Versatile Synthesis of Alternating Conjugated Polymers Enabled by an Open Mechanism
item.description:
CCS Chemistry, Volume 8, Issue 6, Page 2931-2940, June 2026.
DOI:10.31635/ccschem.025.202506240
π-Conjugated polymers (CPs) are essential materials for high-performance optoelectronics, energy conversion, and sensors. However, structural defects in CPs are severely detrimental to the performance of the resulting electronic devices fabricated from them. Here, an efficient polycondensation protocol for the synthesis of alternating CPs with reduced defects was explored, utilizing disulfonium salts as electrophiles via an open mechanism. The generality of this protocol was exemplified by the synthesis of twenty alternating CPs with high molecular weights, including donor–donor (D–D), donor–acceptor (D–A), and acceptor–acceptor (A–A) types. Importantly, this new polymerization strategy significantly reduced the homocoupling side reactions to produce defect-minimized semiconducting CPs with enhanced optoelectronic properties. Thus, this contribution provides new insights into the precise synthesis of CPs.

item.title: Bowl-Shaped Corannulene Donor–Acceptor Emitter Unlocks Host-Modulated Thermally Activated Delayed Fluorescence/Room Temperature Phosphorescence for Oxygen Sensing
item.description:
CCS Chemistry, Ahead of Print.
DOI:10.31635/ccschem.025.202506154
While the study of planar polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) is rich and their properties are well described, the exploitation of curved PAHs such as corannulene derivatives is less established. We report the first example of a corannulene derivative, a donor-substituted corannulene, PXZCor, that exhibited thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF). In degassed toluene, PXZCor emitted bright green TADF, with a delayed fluorescence lifetime, τd, of 67.1 μs. At room temperature, doped films of this derivative in poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) exhibited delayed emission of PXZCor, primarily originating from TADF; as the temperature decreased, phosphorescence gradually became the dominant contributor to the delayed emission. In contrast, when PXZCor was doped into the more rigid host using 1,3-bis(N-carbazolyl)benzene (mCP) as the host, a significant TADF emission was observed at room temperature, and distinct phosphorescence only emerged at temperatures below 200 K. PXZCor could act as a potent O2 sensor using emission intensity and lifetime-based assays. Notably, the performance of PXZCor, in lifetime-based detection, ranked among the most effective TADF compounds reported to date for O2 detection down to concentrations as low as 0.01%. These findings highlight the potential of corannulene materials for optoelectronic applications.
